revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Tagewählkalender (Altes Ägypten)]]
The '''Fate Calendar''' existed in {{ill|Ancient Egypt|en|Ancient Egypt|qid=Q11768|lt=Ancient Egypt|1=Ancient Egypt}} as the so-called {{ill|Destiny|en|Destiny|qid=Q216774|lt=Fate|1=Destiny}} calendar, which classified the {{ill|Moon|en|Moon|qid=Q405|lt=Month|1=Moon}} or {{ill|Year|en|Year|qid=Q577|lt=Year|1=Year}} days in an {{ill|Omen|en|Omen|qid=Q1120735|lt=omen|1=Omen}}-like positive or negative manner and in part provided recommendations for conduct as well as {{ill|Prophecy|ja|予言|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prophecy|qq=}}s. The specific form of day-selection divination merged during the {{ill|Late Period|ja|後期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} with the {{ill|Babylonia|en|Babylonia|qid=Q47690|lt=Babylon|1=Babylonia}} {{ill|Zodiac|en|Zodiac|qid=Q40540|lt=Zodiac|1=Zodiac}} and most likely laid the foundation as an {{ill|Astronomy|en|Astronomy|qid=Q333|lt=astronomical|1=Astronomy}} instrument for the later {{ill|Seven-day planetary week|ja|七曜制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seven-day planetary week|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which also makes {{ill|Astrology|en|Astrology|qid=Q34362|lt=astrological statements|1=Astrology}} about the effects of undertakings on the corresponding days, hours, months, and years.<ref name="L">Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch.'' The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (and others), Copenhagen 2007, pp. 146–147.</ref> == Background == In ancient Egyptian history, the Fate Calendar can be attributed to the {{ill|Middle Kingdom|ja|中王国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Kingdom|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (2010 to 1793 B.C.) and, during the {{ill|New Kingdom|ja|新王国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Kingdom|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1550 to 1069 B.C.), increasingly to the {{ill|Ramesside period|ja|ラムセス朝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1188 to 1076 B.C.)<ref>Wolfgang Helck, Eberhard Otto: ''Kleines Lexikon der Ägyptologie''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 1999, pp. 241–243.</ref> The calendar originating from the Ramesside period, {{ill|pTurin CGT 54016 vso|ja|pTurin CGT 54016 vso|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pTurin CGT 54016 vso|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, titled “From Eternity to Eternity”, is the most completely preserved {{ill|Papyrus|en|Papyrus|qid=Q125576|lt=Papyrus|1=Papyrus}}, consisting of three books. In the second book, all the days of the year, including the {{ill|Heriu-renpet|ja|Heriu-renpet|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heriu-renpet|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the [[:en:Leap month|Leap month]], are listed.<ref name="H153" /> The additional division of the day into thirds is based on {{ill|Egyptian mythology|en|Egyptian mythology|qid=Q205740|lt=Egyptian mythology|1=Egyptian mythology}}, in which the three phases of transformation of the {{ill|Solar deity|en|Solar deity|qid=Q511056|lt=Sun god|1=Solar deity}} {{ill|Ra|en|Ra|qid=Q1252904|lt=Re|1=Ra}} in the {{ill|day sky|ja|昼の空|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/day sky|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} play an important role, and Re assumed several manifestations during the day; shortly before {{ill|Sunrise|en|Sunrise|qid=Q193294|lt=sunrise|1=Sunrise}} as {{ill|Harmachis|ja|ハルマキス|lt=Horus on the Horizon|lt_ja=地平線のホルス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harmachis|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with the first rays of the sun in the {{ill|Mesqet|ja|メスクエト|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mesqet|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as {{ill|Chepri|ja|チェプリ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chepri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, at noon as the {{ill|Horus|en|Horus|qid=Q84122|lt=Horus|1=Horus}}-falcon, and in the evening as the setting sun {{ill|Atum|en|Atum|qid=Q170569|lt=Atum|1=Atum}}. The night, during which Re resided in the interior of the goddess {{ill|Nut (goddess)|en|Nut (goddess)|qid=Q165496|lt=Nut|1=Nut (goddess)}} in the {{ill|Duat|en|Duat|qid=Q871389|lt=Duat|1=Duat}}, was always considered unfavorable.<ref>Wolfgang Helck, Eberhard Otto: ''Lexikon der Ägyptologie.'' Volume 5, Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 1985, column 1088.</ref> Each third of the day was accompanied by a prophecy that classified each period as “good” or “bad.” The division of the day was subject to individual festivals of the gods, which formed the basis for mythological narratives and recommendations as well as fate predictions for the respective period, precisely regulating what was to be done and what was to be refrained from. At the beginning of each year, the responsible Fate Calendar was presented. A well-known example is the fate calendar from the ninth regnal year of {{ill|Amenophis I|ja|アメノフィス I|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenophis I|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which juxtaposes the feast days and the corresponding beginnings of the months of the Egyptian lunar calendar with the corresponding days in the Egyptian administrative calendar. The Fate Calendar demonstrates the relationship of the gods to the months and their assignments. The name of the respective deity thus did not denote the month itself, but the festival dedicated to the gods.<ref>Friedrich-Karl Ginzel: ''Handbuch der mathematischen und technischen Chronologie.'' Volume 1, p. 166.</ref> Over the further course of ancient Egyptian history, these gods evolved into “Lords and Protector Gods of the Months”.<ref>See Hans Bonnet: ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Religionsgeschichte (Früher: Reallexikon der ägyptischen Religionsgeschichte)'', Nikol, Hamburg 2005, p. 470.</ref> {{ill|Herodotus|en|Herodotus|qid=Q26825|lt=Herodotus|1=Herodotus}}’s accounts further corroborate the contents of the religious texts: {{Quote |Text=Furthermore, the Egyptians were the first to determine which month and day were sacred to each god and what fate, which end, and what character those born on this or that day would have. Greek poets also adopted these ideas. And the Egyptians discovered far more omens than all other peoples. When something remarkable occurs, they pay attention to its consequences and record them. In a similar future incident, they believe that the same consequences must recur. |Autor={{ill|Herodotus|en|Herodotus|qid=Q26825|lt=Herodotus|1=Herodotus}}<ref>{{ill|Herodotus|en|Herodotus|qid=Q26825|lt=Herodotus|1=Herodotus}}, ''Histories.'' II, 82.</ref>}} Traditionally, the emergence of astrological concepts was connected with {{ill|Mesopotamia|en|Mesopotamia|qid=Q11767|lt=Mesopotamia|1=Mesopotamia}} and Greece, while Egypt was marginalized. However, the analyzed Egyptian textual material points in a different direction, as do Herodotus’s statements. The Mesopotamian writings initially reached Egypt and merged with religious-astronomical templates from {{ill|Alexandria|en|Alexandria|qid=Q87|lt=Alexandria|1=Alexandria}}. The Egyptian-astrological {{ill|terminology|ja|用語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/terminology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|comment=enwiki is a redirect|1=Term (language)|qid=Q1969448}} is well attested in {{ill|Demotic (Egyptian)|en|Demotic (Egyptian)|qid=Q188519|lt=Demotic|1=Demotic (Egyptian)}}, {{ill|Greek|ja|ギリシャ語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greek|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ギリシア語|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ギリシア語|1=Greek language|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q9129}} and {{ill|Latin|en|Latin|qid=Q397|lt=Latin|1=Latin}} as well as in {{ill|Sanskrit|ja|サンスクリット語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanskrit|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サンスクリット|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サンスクリット|1=Sanskrit|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11059}}. In contrast, these forms are unknown in the Mesopotamian realm. Formulations and sentence structures also refer to the typical patterns in the {{ill|Pyramid Texts|en|Pyramid Texts|qid=Q645882|lt=Pyramid Texts|1=Pyramid Texts}}. The findings of the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|Book of Nut|qid=Q2005559|lt=Book of Nut|1=Book of Nut}} show further similarities. In addition, the fact that the ancient astrological {{ill|treatises|ja|論文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/treatises|1=thesis|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q124212544}} always attribute the teachings to Egyptian authors comes into play.<ref name="L" />
== See also == * {{ill|Fate Calendar (Mesopotamia)|ja|メソポタミアの運命暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fate Calendar (Mesopotamia)|2=de|3=Tagewählkalender (Mesopotamien)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== Sources == * {{ill|pKahun XVII|ja|pKahun XVII|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pKahun XVII|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="H153">See Wolfgang Helck, Eberhard Otto: ''Lexikon der Ägyptologie.'' Volume 6, Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 1986, columns 153–154.</ref> (Middle Kingdom) * {{ill|oTurin CGT 57304|ja|oTurin CGT 57304|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oTurin CGT 57304|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="H153" /> (New Kingdom) * {{ill|pLeiden I 346|ja|pLeiden I 346|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pLeiden I 346|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="H153" /> (New Kingdom) * {{ill|Deir el-Medineh|ja|デイル・エル・メディネh|lt=Ostracon from Deir el-Medineh|lt_ja=デイル・エル・メディネhのオストラコン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Deir el-Medineh|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="H153" /> (Ramesside period) * {{ill|pBM 10184 rto|ja|pBM 10184 rto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pBM 10184 rto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, ({{ill|pSallier IV rto|ja|pSallier IV rto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pSallier IV rto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})<ref name="H153" /> (Ramesside period) * {{ill|pBM 10474 vso|ja|pBM 10474 vso|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pBM 10474 vso|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="H153" /> (Ramesside period) * {{ill|pKairo 86637|ja|pKairo 86637|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pKairo 86637|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="H153" /> (Ramesside period) * {{ill|pTurin CGT 54016 vso|ja|pTurin CGT 54016 vso|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pTurin CGT 54016 vso|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="H153" /> (Ramesside period) == Literature == * {{ill|Hans Bonnet (Egyptologist)|ja|Hans Bonnet|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hans Bonnet (Egyptologist)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: ''Lexicon of Egyptian Religious History.'' (Formerly: ''Reallexicon of Egyptian Religious History.'') Nikol, Hamburg 2005, ISBN 3-937872-08-6. * {{ill|Friedrich Karl Ginzel|ja|Friedrich Karl Ginzel|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Friedrich Karl Ginzel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: ''Handbook of Mathematical and Technical Chronology. Volume 1 – Chronology of Babylonians, Egyptians, Muslims, Persians, Indians, Southeast Asians, Chinese, Japanese and Central Americans.'' Deutsche Buch-Ex- und Import, Leipzig 1958 (Reprint Leipzig 1906). * {{ill|Wolfgang Helck|ja|Wolfgang Helck|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wolfgang Helck|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Eberhard Otto (Egyptologist)|ja|Eberhard Otto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eberhard Otto (Egyptologist)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: ''Small Lexicon of Egyptology.'' Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 1999, ISBN 3-447-04027-0. * Wolfgang Helck, Eberhard Otto: ''Lexicon of Egyptology.'' Volume 6, Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 1986, ISBN 3-447-02663-4. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|ja|Alexandra von Lieven|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: ''Outline of the Course of the Stars – The So-called Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (and others), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5. == References == <references /> {{Navigation box Egyptian Astronomy|qq=}}
{{translated page|de|Tagewählkalender (Altes Ägypten)|version=255269647|comment=Imported full de history}}
